posts.title: Korean Hemorrhagic Fever in the Korean War
posts.excerpt: How Korean War trench warfare, rodent ecology, and Hantaan virus turned a mysterious military outbreak into hantavirus history.
posts.search_excerpt: Between Spring of 1951 and the armistice of July 1953, an unnamed disease infected UN soldiers among the ridge lines and rice paddies of central Korea.
posts.seo_title: Korean War Hantavirus: Hemorrhagic Fever History
posts.seo_description: How Korean War trench warfare, rodent ecology, and Hantaan virus turned a mysterious military outbreak into hantavirus history.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about population and disease?

The different blood types carry different susceptibilities to specific pathogens with cholera, norovirus, some respiratory viruses, and others showing ABO-dependent susceptibility patterns.
The major genetic risk factor for celiac disease (a variant at HLA-DQ) went from about 0% to roughly 20% in 4,000 years during and after the spread of wheat farming across West Eurasia.
With over 900 ancient genomes ranging from the Mesolithic to the Viking Age, they found the oldest carriers of this mutation dating back more than 6,700 years in the Western Eurasian Steppe.

posts.title: Disease on the Oregon Trail: Cholera, Dysentery, and Death
posts.excerpt: Disease on the Oregon Trail, from cholera and dysentery to overland migration, sanitation, graves, and the epidemiology of nineteenth-century westward travel.
posts.search_excerpt: “Started out soon this morning, passed several graves, we hear that it is sickly on the route…” noted Lodisa Frizzell in May of 1852 in her journal documenting her family’s journey from Illinois to Wyoming.

posts.title: When Worlds Collided: Disease and Population Collapse in the Americas After 1492
posts.excerpt: The story of disease in the Americas post-1492 is usually told in a blunt manner, with Europeans arriving, Old World pathogens hitching a ride, and then causing a total collapse of the Indigenous Populations.
posts.search_excerpt: The story of disease in the Americas post-1492 is usually told in a blunt manner, with Europeans arriving, Old World pathogens hitching a ride, and then causing a total collapse of the Indigenous Populations.
posts.seo_title: Disease and Population Collapse After 1492
posts.seo_description: A careful look at disease, colonial disruption, Indigenous population collapse, Old World pathogens, and the limits of simple post-1492 stories.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about population and outbreak?
choices: It’s possible the first smallpox outbreak occurred on the in 1507 , when disease was devastating the populations of the island.

The story of disease in the Americas post-1492 is usually told in a blunt manner, with Europeans arriving, Old World pathogens hitching a ride, and then causing a total collapse of the Indigenous Populations.
In 1493 , both native Taíno people and the Spanish in the town of La Isabela, Hispaniola succumbed to a likely influenza outbreak, with fever, malaise, and high mortality rates.
It is safe to say that population collapse was well underway by the time of the 1518-1519 (and first confirmed) smallpox epidemic.
flashcards.answer: It’s possible the first smallpox outbreak occurred on the in 1507 , when disease was devastating the populations of the island.

posts.title: The Disease Ecology of Pre-Contact Americas (Part 1: North and Central America)
posts.excerpt: Before 1492, the indigenous people of the Americas lived in a long-term isolation from the vast reservoir that was Eurasia’s disease pool.
posts.search_excerpt: Before 1492, the indigenous people of the Americas lived in a long-term isolation from the vast reservoir that was Eurasia’s disease pool.
posts.seo_title: Pre-Contact North and Central American Disease Ecology
posts.seo_description: Disease ecology in North and Central America before 1492, including isolation from Eurasian disease pools and Indigenous health context.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about reservoir and disease?
choices: Before 1492, the indigenous people of the Americas lived in a long-term isolation from the vast reservoir that was Eurasia’s disease pool.
Due to being more dispersed and less commonly packed densely into large urban centers, they largely avoided the crowd-amplified diseases of the Old World like smallpox and measles.
That said, a variety of infectious diseases were endemic (continually present in the area) and there was the occasional epidemic outbreak in pre-Columbian societies.
Paleopathology, ancient DNA, and indigenous oral/early colonial accounts reveal just what was going on in the ecological landscape of infectious disease in the Americas.
flashcards.answer: Before 1492, the indigenous people of the Americas lived in a long-term isolation from the vast reservoir that was Eurasia’s disease pool.

posts.title: The Ghosts of Meals Past: What We’ve Learned from Fossilized Poop
posts.excerpt: When construction first broke ground for the Lloyds Bank building in York in 1972, within a moist layer of peat was something each of the crew would have seen countless times before, although never one like this.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about disease and Viking Age evidence?

Now it’s on display at the Jorvik Viking Center, in what is likely one of the least glamorous museum spotlights imaginable.
To their surprise, and likely their amusement, the lump of 9 th century waste would become one of the crown jewels in Viking archeology, giving us a gut-level record of diet, disease, and daily life.
Some 1200 years ago a Viking squatted down and left us, from what I can tell, what is still the largest known human coprolite.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about smallpox and malaria?
choices: Given where it, and we, originate, malaria likely holds the distinction of being the oldest sustained human-pathogen relationship, with evidence of the causal Plasmodium being present before homo sapiens .
Certified as eradicated across the globe in 1980, the smallpox virus has a more mysterious origin, with it still not being fully understood.
We can’t reconstruct some Bronze Age cholera outbreak when all we’ve got is clay tablet with something like “and the god smote those along the river” tapped into it.

But we can be confident in some things, like smallpox having killed more than the plague or that malaria’ death dwarf influenza deaths in the cumulative toll, but any precise number is just an educated guess…

posts.seo_title: GLP-1 Drugs and Migraine: Evidence Guide
posts.seo_description: What GLP-1 agonists might mean for migraine, headache biology, metabolic drugs, clinical evidence, and what remains speculative.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about evidence and migraine?
choices: The evidence for migraine relief is still thin but biologically plausible, and in medicine that’s usually where revolutions start.

posts.title: Psychedelics for Anxiety: What the New LSD Trial Does and Doesn’t Change
posts.excerpt: In the Middle Ages, ergot was a curse with a Latin name.
posts.search_excerpt: In the Middle Ages, ergot was a curse with a Latin name.
posts.seo_title: Psychedelics for Anxiety: LSD Trial Evidence
posts.seo_description: What a new LSD trial does and does not show about psychedelics for anxiety, trial design, clinical endpoints, and evidence limits.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about phase multicenter randomized double-blind?
choices: • Claim you can quote: A single 100 µg dose of MM120 produced a medium, clinically meaningful reduction in GAD symptoms without trial-provided psychotherapy at four weeks that was somewhat sustained up to 12…

A phase 2b, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial gave one supervised dose of either 25, 50, 100, or 200 µg to adults with moderate to severe GAD and followed them for 12 weeks.
The result was that 100–200 µg beat placebo by ~5–6 HAM-A points at 4 weeks, with durable separation to week 12 and response/remission rates roughly double placebo.
This is a pretty big milestone for drug-forward psychedelic pharmacotherapy: a signal that survives without the expensive therapy wrapper.

posts.title: Paper Mills are Poisoning Science
posts.excerpt: You can now pay a few hundred bucks to publish your name on a scientific study.
posts.search_excerpt: You can now pay a few hundred bucks to publish your name on a scientific study.
posts.seo_title: Paper Mills and Scientific Fraud
posts.seo_description: How paper mills poison science, why fake studies spread, and what scientific publishing loses when quantity beats credibility.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about Dan Ariely?
choices: A new paper in PNAS titled “ The entities enabling scientific fraud at scale are large, resilient, and growing rapidly ” shows that this goes beyond a handful of rogue researchers fudging data a la Dan Ariely.
Think something like a college that was deemed a diploma mill, but instead of selling fake degrees, they sell authorship on peer-reviewed articles.
And because the incentives can be skewed, favoring quantity over quality, speed over substance, there are always editors, journals, and brokers ready to make it happen.
Examples given are a paper about roasting hazelnuts appearing in a journal on HIV/AIDS and an article on malware detection showing up in a special education journal.
flashcards.answer: A new paper in PNAS titled “ The entities enabling scientific fraud at scale are large, resilient, and growing rapidly ” shows that this goes beyond a handful of rogue researchers fudging data a la Dan Ariely.

posts.title: Memetics is dead, long live memetics
posts.excerpt: On stealth memetics, viral ideas, and why the term may have died while the logic behind it lived on.
posts.search_excerpt: On stealth memetics, viral ideas, and why the term may have died while the logic behind it lived on.
posts.seo_title: Memetics After Memetics
posts.seo_description: Why the word memetics faded while viral ideas, imitation, information spread, and cultural transmission kept shaping public life.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about outbreak?
choices: You also see co-infections in the form of “memeplexes” (bundles of ideas that increase one another’s transmissibility, like cultures or religions).
What started as a clever metaphor for cultural evolution seemed more like an addition to the outbreak modeling books and lectures I had read and been to.
Paper titles like Epidemic Spreading in Scale-Free Networks 1 show that the language of information as contagion is alive and well.
Some memes spread fast and wide, like measles of COVID, with short incubation periods and high transmission rates (think celebrity gossip, viral videos, or tiktok dances).

posts.title: The Poison Nobody Could Agree On: The Unsolved 1951 Outbreak of Pont-Saint-Esprit
posts.excerpt: In August of 1951, something toxic ripped through the town of Pont-Saint-Esprit in southern France.
posts.search_excerpt: In August of 1951, something toxic ripped through the town of Pont-Saint-Esprit in southern France.
posts.seo_title: Pont-Saint-Esprit Poisoning Outbreak of 1951
posts.seo_description: The unsolved 1951 Pont-Saint-Esprit outbreak, ergot theories, poisoning claims, CIA rumors, and why retrospective diagnosis stays hard.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about evidence?
choices: The evidence boils down to a few declassified documents that tie Pont-Saint-Esprit to Frank Olson, a US Army biochemist who died under suspicious circumstances in 1953 after being dosed with LSD a week earlier.
Albarelli on the other hand heavily champions the CIA theory in his 2005, twelve-hundred-page book A Terrible Mistake: The Murder of Frank Olson and the CIA’s Secret Cold War Experiments .
The truth is, we don’t know, and there hasn’t really been any more evidence coming forward that makes one theory more likely than the others.
Investigative journalist Hank Albarelli published a book claiming the outbreak in southern France was part of a covert experiment under the name of MK-NAOMI (a cousin of MK-ULTRA).
flashcards.answer: The evidence boils down to a few declassified documents that tie Pont-Saint-Esprit to Frank Olson, a US Army biochemist who died under suspicious circumstances in 1953 after being dosed with LSD a week earlier.

posts.title: Migraine: What We Know and Who It Hits Hardest
posts.excerpt: Migraine is one of the most disabling conditions we know of. Here's what the data says about who gets it, who doesn't get help, and why we still don't quite understand it.

posts.seo_title: Migraine Epidemiology: Who Gets Hit Hardest
posts.seo_description: Migraine is disabling and unevenly treated. Here is what the data says about who gets it, who lacks help, and why uncertainty remains.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about population and migraine?

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about things worse average timing?
choices: To make things worse for the average user, the timing and dosages in commercial products are often inaccurate, with some doseges being off by as much as 478% from the label 3 .
This means melatonin tends to work best when it’s used to shift or reinforce that internal circadian clock, not as a last-minute fix taken after midnight because a person can’t fall asleep.
That’s where the problem is, because most people are popping melatonin for general or transient insomnia, which is really a misuse of it.

If you’ve got insomnia that is chronic or tied to anxiety, racing thoughts, or stress, something like CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia) has quite a bit of evidence as a successful option.

posts.title: When the Influencer’s Claims are Stronger than the Evidence: A Look at Rhonda Patrick’s Vitamin D Claims
posts.excerpt: By now, more than 200,000 people on Twitter and over 30,000 on YouTube1 have seen the new video of Dr. Rhonda Patrick explaining how vitamin D supplements can cut your dementia risk by 40%2. That’s a bold, hopeful claim based on an observational study of over 12,000 people. And I think she means well. But how true is it?

posts.seo_title: Vitamin D, Dementia, and Rhonda Patrick’s Claims
posts.seo_description: An evidence check on Rhonda Patrick’s vitamin D and dementia claims, observational studies, confounding, and supplement certainty.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about population and dementia?
choices: And I think she means well, even adding an asterisk to the video thumbnail to say “*in observational studies”.

By acting like the evidence nearly follows the same trend over and over, she gives the impression that the causal link between vitamin D and dementia in the overall population is settled, but it’s not.

Tracing the mechanistic threads without checking them against outcome data risks falling into the same trap that has plagued Alzheimer’s and Dementia research for years: chasing elegant ideas that never end…
Saying vitamin D “reduces dementia risk by 40%” makes it sound like a causal guarantee, but that’s not what the evidence says.
flashcards.answer: By acting like the evidence nearly follows the same trend over and over, she gives the impression that the causal link between vitamin D and dementia in the overall population is settled, but it’s not.

posts.title: Creatine Is Underrated (By Those Who Would Benefit Most)
posts.excerpt: Creatine is having another moment in the news.
posts.search_excerpt: Creatine is having another moment in the news.
posts.seo_title: Creatine Benefits and Evidence
posts.seo_description: Why creatine may be underrated for people who could benefit, including muscle, aging, cognition claims, and supplement evidence limits.

flashcards.question: Which statement best matches the essay's point about risk?
choices: The warning earlier is more in line with the conservative view the medical establishment has against recommending something that hasn’t been tested in a specific population.
A recent Wall Street Journal article framed creatine as a trendy, potentially serious supplement for brain health, bone density, biological aging, sleep, and even mood.
So while the internet continues to debate whether creatine helps you get that extra rep in at the gym or do some cognitive brain game faster, I want to zoom out a bit.
If you’re already eating a lot of red meat, you may be near that saturation point and further supplementation, while not risky, may be rather pointless.
flashcards.answer: If you’re already eating a lot of red meat, you may be near that saturation point and further supplementation, while not risky, may be rather pointless.
